title: Fix subcircuit missing-trace net labels to use trace display labels
description: Fixes incorrect net label generation for subcircuit ports with missing traces, ensuring user-assigned labels are respected.
body:
This fixes an issue where schematic net labels generated for subcircuit ports with missing/routed-away schematic traces fell back to selector-derived labels like J_LEFT_GP2 or U1_GPIO2, even when the connected <trace /> had an explicit label such as name, displayName, or schDisplayLabel.

The missing-trace label path was not using the same net-label resolution logic as the normal routed trace label path. This meant subcircuit boundary connections could ignore user-assigned trace labels and generate implicit port labels instead.

The fix follows the existing codebase pattern by using getNetNameFromPorts() in insertNetLabelsForPortsMissingTrace, matching the logic already used by applyNetLabelPlacements. That helper resolves assigned trace labels through _getSchematicNetLabelText(), preserving the established precedence: schDisplayLabel ?? displayName ?? name

Only assigned display labels are promoted into the missing-trace fallback chain, so implicit port labels are still used when no trace label was explicitly provided.

title: Fix: repro14 where exposingNets didn't create `pcb_trace` but the schematic showed connection

body: For these net-to-net traces, it created logical source_trace entries, but when building PCB routes it did not follow those nets far enough to discover the chip pins behind them. So it saw no physical points to route between, and produced no pcb_trace.

title: fix: size legend columns to available width instead of a fixed 3-column cap
description: Fixes the legend layout in the simulation oscilloscope to dynamically adjust the number of columns based on the available width, preventing unnecessary wrapping of channel cards.
body:
## Summary

The simulation oscilloscope legend was hard-coded to a maximum of **3 columns** (`SCOPE_LEGEND_MAX_COLUMNS = 3`), regardless of how wide the rendered SVG actually was. On scopes with 4+ trace channels this forced the channel cards to wrap into an unbalanced extra row even when there was ample horizontal space to lay them out on fewer rows — wasting vertical space and pushing the legend further below the plot than necessary.

This PR makes the legend column count **derive from the available width**, so the legend packs as many fixed-width channel cards per row as physically fit.

## Problem (before)

`getScopeLegendGridLayout()` ignored `containerWidth` for the column count and capped columns at 3:

- **4 channels** → forced into `3 + 1` (two rows, one orphaned card)
- **5 channels** → forced into `3 + 2` (two rows)

…even when the SVG was wide enough to fit all cards on fewer rows.

## Fix (after)

Columns are now computed from the container width using the real card geometry (`CARD_WIDTH = 118`, `CARD_GAP = 18`):

```ts
const columnsThatFit = Math.floor(
  (containerWidth + SCOPE_LEGEND_CARD_GAP) /
    (SCOPE_LEGEND_CARD_WIDTH + SCOPE_LEGEND_CARD_GAP),
)
const columns = Math.max(1, Math.min(columnsThatFit, graphCount))
```

For the standard `width: 500` scope render:

- **4 channels** → single row of `4` (was `3 + 1`)
- **5 channels** → `4 + 1` (was `3 + 2`)

The legend stays centered within the graph's viewBox — no overflow — and is clamped to a minimum of one column so narrow containers degrade gracefully.

## Reproduction & test

Added `tests/sim/simulation-graph-four-channel-example.test.tsx`: a placeholder resistor schematic plus **four oscilloscope trace channels** (VO / L1 / L2 / IL). It exercises the exact >3-channel case that previously wrapped, asserts the legend renders all four channels, and locks the corrected single-row layout in with an SVG snapshot.

## Changes

- `lib/sim/simulation-graph-svg/create-legend/get-scope-legend-grid-layout.ts` — replace the fixed `SCOPE_LEGEND_MAX_COLUMNS = 3` cap with a width-based fit.
- `tests/sim/simulation-graph-four-channel-example.test.tsx` — new four-channel scope reproduction + snapshot.
- Updated `four-channel-scope-legend` / `five-channel-scope-legend` snapshots to reflect the tighter, correctly-sized layout.

## Testing

- `bun test tests/sim/` → 17 pass / 0 fail
- `npx tsc --noEmit` → clean
- `biome format` → clean

title: Add pcb_board template support for board-level Circuit JSON conversion
description: Adds support for converting board-level Circuit JSON with a pcb_board template to tscircuit format, enabling richer circuit designs beyond the existing chip conversion path.
body:
## Summary

Advances [#8](https://github.com/tscircuit/circuit-json-to-tscircuit/issues/8) by adding the missing `pcb_board` conversion path in `circuit-json-to-tscircuit`.

Issue #8 is about supporting templates beyond `<Chip />`. The issue example includes a full board-shaped Circuit JSON with a `pcb_board`, placed components, and traces. Before this change, inputs of that shape still fell through the reusable `<chip>` conversion path, so the converter had no board-level template to start from.

This PR adds that board template layer: when the input contains `pcb_board`, the converter now generates a tscircuit `<board>` with mapped board props. The existing chip conversion path remains unchanged for footprint/component-import inputs that do not contain `pcb_board`.

## Scope relative to #8

This is a partial implementation of #8.

Included here:
- board-level dispatch based on presence of `pcb_board`
- `<board>` template generation
- mapping supported `pcb_board` fields to `BoardProps`
- regression coverage for board-shaped Circuit JSON

Follow-up work still needed for full #8 coverage:
- emit placed component children such as `<resistor>` / `<capacitor>` from `source_component`
- emit connection children such as `<trace>` from `source_trace`
- reconstruct the complete multi-component example shape described in #8

## What changed

- Added `getBoardUsingTemplate` in `lib/get-board-using-template.ts`.
- Updated `convertCircuitJsonToTscircuit` to route Circuit JSON with `pcb_board` to the board converter.
- Kept the existing `<chip>` conversion path unchanged for non-board inputs.
- Reused existing helper patterns (`su()` and `mmStr()`) so the new path matches the current converter style.

## Data-model mapping

The new board template emits each supported board prop only when present:

- `width` -> `width`
- `height` -> `height`
- `outline` -> `outline`
- `thickness` -> `thickness`
- `num_layers` -> `layers`
- `material` -> `material`
- `solder_mask_color` -> `solderMaskColor`
- `silkscreen_color` -> `silkscreenColor`
- `anchor_position` -> `boardAnchorPosition`
- `anchor_alignment` -> `boardAnchorAlignment`

Design-rule and clearance fields such as `min_trace_width` are intentionally not emitted because there is no matching `BoardProps` surface for them today.

## Tests

- Added an inline snapshot for generated `<board>` code.
- Added a regression test covering board-shaped Circuit JSON input.
- Used a non-square `20mm x 10mm` fixture so width/height swaps cannot pass silently.
- Rendered the generated code with `runTscircuitCode` and snapshot-tested the resulting PCB SVG to verify the board path compiles and round-trips.
- Re-ran existing chip conversion tests to confirm the non-board path is unchanged.

## Why this helps #8

Issue #8 starts from the problem that the converter is missing templates beyond `<Chip />`. This PR establishes the board-level template needed for those richer conversions, so follow-up work can add component and trace children onto a valid `<board>` output instead of trying to force full-board Circuit JSON through the chip path.

body:
## Summary

- Make `AutoroutingPipelineSolver` point to `AutoroutingPipelineSolver7_MultiGraph`.
- Keep explicit Pipeline4 and Pipeline7 exports unchanged.
- Update snapshots that now exercise Pipeline7 through the default export.

## Why Snapshots Differ

- The checked `e2e3` fixture has no detected components, so Pipeline7's component path is not the cause. Pipeline4 and Pipeline7 matched through `highDensityRouteSolver`.
- First measured divergence is `highDensityForceImproveSolver`.
- Pipeline4 runs `totalStepsPerNode: Math.max(20, Math.round(60 * cms.effort))`.
  - Author: Shibo
  - Commit: https://github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit-autorouter/commit/a2d55ae05333b017ca140f3deb723760f6479f0a
  - Message: `Integrate HighDensityForceImproveSolver (#876)`
- Pipeline7 runs `totalStepsPerNode: Math.max(12, Math.round(20 * cms.effort))`.
  - Author: Severin Ibarluzea
  - Commit: https://github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit-autorouter/commit/40fb425cbfa5f0f8cd6aa993eeaf12a43302b653
  - Message: `avoid exploding topology size from BGAs (#1313)`
- Pipeline7 also has extra high-density grow/shrink options.
  - Author: Severin Ibarluzea
  - Commit: https://github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit-autorouter/commit/485bf6297
  - These did not change `e2e3` output before `highDensityForceImproveSolver` in the comparison.

## Validation

- `bun run build`
- Focused default-export snapshot suite passed: 24 pass, 1 skip.
- Full `bun test` was run. After updating default-output snapshots, four unrelated timeout-only tests still reproduced individually:

description: This PR changes the output of the schematic file generation to return a list of schematic files instead of a single file, allowing for future support of multi-sheet schematics without requiring structural changes.
body:
### Proposal
This sets up `CircuitJsonToKicadSchConverter` to return schematic files as a list instead of assuming there is always only one `.kicad_sch` file.

For now, it still returns one file, so behavior stays the same.

Later, this API can support schematic sheets by returning multiple files, for example:

- `circuit.kicad_sch`
- `Sheet_1.kicad_sch`
- `Sheet_2.kicad_sch`

The site export now uses this file-list API, so it will not need another structure change when multi-sheet schematic export is implemented.

title: fix: include the anchor shape of KiCad custom pads
description: Fixes the omission of anchor shapes in KiCad custom pads, ensuring accurate copper representation in converted footprints.
body:
Closes #159

Fix for the bug repro'd in #157. (Builds on that branch — if #157 lands first, this narrows to just the fix.)

### What was wrong
A KiCad `smd custom` pad's copper is the **union of its anchor shape** (the rect/circle at the pad's `(at …)` + `(size …)`) **and its primitive graphics**. The converter emitted only the primitives and dropped the anchor (`process-pads.ts` returned right after the primitives).

For **SOT-89-3** the anchor *is* the collector-tab neck (`1.475×0.9` reaching toward the leads), so the converted tab lost it — rendering the real footprint with `kicad-cli` shows a T-shaped tab; the converted one was just the wide rectangle.

### The fix
Emit the anchor (rect/circle, honoring rotation) **only when it adds copper** — i.e. when it extends past the primitives' footprint. Skip it otherwise:
- KiCad's near-zero placeholder anchors (`size ~0.001`, used when the shape is fully defined by primitives, e.g. paste apertures), and
- anchors that sit entirely within the primitives (which would just be an overlapping duplicate pad).

This keeps the blast radius to footprints that were genuinely missing copper. Besides SOT-89 it also restores dropped anchor copper on **gmsl-serializer**, **oculink-to-pcie-adapter** and **usb-c-power-adapter** (snapshots updated; corne / joule-thief / ov9281 etc. are unchanged).

### Tests
- Flips the #157 repro (`custom-pad-anchor-shape`) to a passing `test()`.
- Updates `pad-id-uniqueness` (custom pads now also yield their anchor pad, so 2 → 4).

Transforms the fabrication UI into a complete operator workspace by supporting Circuit JSON imports, dedicated stage execution pages, and end-to-end fabrication job management.

This PR includes:

* Direct Circuit JSON file import for creating fabrication jobs
* Automatic LightBurn (`.lbrn`) generation from imported Circuit JSON
* Dedicated job stages workspace separated from the dashboard
* Streamlined dashboard focused on job creation and job selection
* End-to-end fabrication job creation from uploaded Circuit JSON files
* Shared dashboard state reused across dashboard and stage execution pages
* Automatic board metadata extraction from imported Circuit JSON
* Improved navigation between job management and operator workflows
* Enhanced upload experience with file selection feedback
* Reusable dashboard state type for shared application routing

This establishes a complete fabrication workflow where users can import Circuit JSON files, generate fabrication jobs, and transition directly into a dedicated operator workspace for executing fabrication stages and machine operations.

Transforms the dashboard from a monitoring interface into an interactive fabrication workstation by integrating job creation, LightBurn file generation, carrier controls, laser operations, and burn-run inspection.

This PR includes:

* Fabrication job creation from sample Circuit JSON designs
* Automatic generation and packaging of stage-specific LightBurn (`.lbrn`) files
* End-to-end integration with the fake fabrication server's job creation API
* Operator controls for carrier clamping, movement, rotation, and orientation
* Laser origin configuration and stage burn execution
* Stage completion workflow integration
* Burn history listing and burn-run inspection
* Shared API action layer encapsulating fabrication operations
* LBRN file generation utilities with validation of required fabrication outputs
* Dashboard UI for fabrication job creation and machine operation

This establishes a complete operator workflow that spans fabrication job creation, machine control, laser execution, and burn-run management, providing the foundation for an end-to-end interactive laser fabrication experience.

title: Refine PCB Feed Animation with Percentage-Based Travel Controls and CAD-Aligned Motion
description: Refines PCB feed animation by replacing rotation-based travel with a normalized percentage-driven motion model and aligning board movement with the CAD coordinate system.
body:
**PR Description**
Refines PCB feed animation by replacing rotation-based travel with a normalized percentage-driven motion model and aligning board movement with the CAD coordinate system.

This PR includes:

* Percentage-based PCB feed controls (`-100%` to `100%`)
* CAD-aligned PCB translation along the board feed axis
* Updated `getCadPcbFeedOffset` API using normalized feed progress
* Simplified feed-distance calculation independent of wheel rotation
* Updated interactive controls reflecting feed percentage instead of degrees
* Regression tests validating normalized feed offsets across the full travel range

This improves the mechanical animation model by decoupling PCB travel from wheel rotation, providing a more intuitive and deterministic control interface for future fabrication simulation workflows.

title: Introduce Feeder-Driven PCB Motion for CAD-Based Laser Fabrication Bench Animation
description: Adds feeder-wheel-driven PCB translation to the GLB bench model, connecting feeder control input to visible board movement inside the rotating jig assembly.
body:
Adds feeder-wheel-driven PCB translation to the GLB bench model, connecting feeder control input to visible board movement inside the rotating jig assembly.

This PR includes:

* Propagation of feeder wheel rotation into the 3D bench model
* CAD-space PCB feed offset calculation from feeder rotation
* X-axis PCB translation tied to feeder wheel revolutions
* PCB-only motion inside the shared jig assembly pivot group
* Public export for `getCadPcbFeedOffset`
* Regression tests validating PCB feed offset at 0°, 180°, and 360°

This makes the bench animation more mechanically meaningful by linking feeder wheel input to deterministic PCB feed motion, laying the groundwork for coordinated fabrication motion simulation.

title: Introduce Pivot-Based Jig Assembly Animation for the 3D Laser Fabrication Bench
description: Establishes a reusable mechanical animation framework for the laser fabrication bench, enabling physically accurate assembly motion and providing a foundation for future kinematics, machine simulation, and coordinated component animations.
body:
Introduces a pivot-driven animation system for the laser fabrication bench, allowing the jig assembly to rotate as a mechanically linked unit while exposing reusable CAD transform utilities.

This PR includes:

* Pivot-based jig assembly rotation using a shared mechanical pivot
* Grouped jig assembly containing the jig, PCB, feeder wheels, and motor gear
* Configurable jig rotation propagated into the 3D model
* Reusable CAD axis rotation helpers for X, Y, and Z rotations
* Dedicated CAD jig rotation utility
* Public exports for jig assembly metadata and transform helpers
* Shared model manifest describing rotating assembly membership
* Updated interactive controls supporting full 360° jig rotation
* Regression tests covering pivot configuration, assembly membership, and CAD rotation helpers

This establishes a reusable mechanical animation framework for the laser fabrication bench, enabling physically accurate assembly motion and providing a foundation for future kinematics, machine simulation, and coordinated component animations.

title: fix(sch): create cross-sheet net labels per schematic sheet
description: Fixes the issue where cross-subcircuit net labels collapsed to a single label for the whole net instead of one per sheet, ensuring each schematic sheet receives its own label for shared connections.
body:
For a connection spanning multiple schematic sheets (e.g. a shared I2C bus wired with direct pin-to-pin traces), the auto-generated cross-subcircuit net label collapsed to a single label for the whole net instead of one per sheet — and that
label was stamped with the rendering group's sheet while positioned at a pin from a different sheet. So a controller bus label like `U1_PA0` showed up on the *sensor* sheet, and the other sheets on the bus got no label at all.

## Fix
Made net-label creation/de-duplication in `insertNetLabelsForPortsMissingTrace` sheet-aware: each port's sheet is resolved from its own `schematic_component`, and that sheet id is used for the dedup lookups, the orphan-cleanup, and the inserted label's `schematic_sheet_id`. Every sheet a net reaches now gets its own label anchored to its own pin. Single-sheet circuits are unchanged.

title: Update autorouter, support trace having connected obstacles, remove trace trimming logic, fix subcircuit handling
description: What Changed This is a code-clarity follow-up built on top of 2549. Documented the SRJ contract in getSimpleRouteJsonFromCircuitJson: connections are routing work for the current autorouter, while traces are existing copper to preserve. Removed the breakout-point route trimming helper for preserved child subcircuit traces. Preserved child subcircuit traces now keep their original route geometry and carry connectedTo metadata derived from the connectivity map, source trace, attached netsports, and route endpoint PCB ports. Updated the local SRJ trace type so core can pass traces.connectedTo through to the updated tscircuitcapacity-autorouter. Renamed the local routed-trace bookkeeping so it is clear which source_traces are already represented by preserved SRJ traces. Renamed _createTracesForExposedNets to _createTracesForExposedConnections. Added Trace._exposesSubcircuitConnection and set it on generated exposed-connection helper traces, so re-render protection only matches traces created by this helper. Removed generated name values from exposed-connection helper traces. They now keep only displayName, because these traces are generated routing intent, not user-named children. Updated subcircuit duplicate-name DRC so immediate non-subcircuit children generally need unique names, but same-named traces are allowed when every duplicate has the same non-empty subcircuit_connectivity_map_key. Added focused unit coverage for duplicate trace-name connectivity keys and preserved SRJ trace connectivity metadata.  Why The subcircuitSRJ interaction is easy to misread because the same circuit-json data can represent two different things: a child subcircuit route that already exists and should be preserved as SRJ traces parent-board routing intent that still needs to be emitted as SRJ connections With traces.connectedTo support in the autorouter, core no longer needs to trim preserved trace geometry around breakout points to make parent routing work. The preserved trace stays geometrically accurate, and the autorouter gets the same-net relationship explicitly through SRJ connectivity metadata. For naming, the invariant is: generated exposed-connection helper traces should not set name user-named immediate children should not collide across different things same-named traces may coexist only when the connectivity map proves they are the same underlying connection nested subcircuits remain separately scoped  Validation .node_modules.binbiome format --write libutilsautoroutinggetSimpleRouteJsonFromCircuitJson.ts libutilsautoroutingSimpleRouteJson.ts testsutilsautoroutingsimple-route-json-ignores-routed-subcircuit-traces.test.ts bun test testsutilsautoroutingsimple-route-json-ignores-routed-subcircuit-traces.test.ts testscomponentsprimitive-componentsgroup-duplicate-trace-name-connectivity-key.test.ts bunx tsc --noEmit still fails on existing unrelated baseline errors around graph display props, source_unnamed_trace_warning, and the circuit-to-svg convertCircuitJsonToStackedSchematicSheetsSvg export. Previously attempted: bun test testssubcircuitssubcircuit-exposed-nets-copper-pour01.test.tsx The fixture-backed exposed-net test is still blocked at module load by the existing circuit-to-svg export issue: convertCircuitJsonToStackedSchematicSheetsSvg is not exported.
body:
## What Changed

This is a code-clarity follow-up built on top of #2549.

- Documented the SRJ contract in `getSimpleRouteJsonFromCircuitJson`: `connections` are routing work for the current autorouter, while `traces` are existing copper to preserve.
- Removed the breakout-point route trimming helper for preserved child subcircuit traces.
- Preserved child subcircuit traces now keep their original route geometry and carry `connectedTo` metadata derived from the connectivity map, source trace, attached nets/ports, and route endpoint PCB ports.
- Updated the local SRJ trace type so core can pass `traces[*].connectedTo` through to the updated `@tscircuit/capacity-autorouter`.
- Renamed the local routed-trace bookkeeping so it is clear which `source_trace`s are already represented by preserved SRJ traces.
- Renamed `_createTracesForExposedNets` to `_createTracesForExposedConnections`.
- Added `Trace._exposesSubcircuitConnection` and set it on generated exposed-connection helper traces, so re-render protection only matches traces created by this helper.
- Removed generated `name` values from exposed-connection helper traces. They now keep only `displayName`, because these traces are generated routing intent, not user-named children.
- Updated subcircuit duplicate-name DRC so immediate non-subcircuit children generally need unique names, but same-named traces are allowed when every duplicate has the same non-empty `subcircuit_connectivity_map_key`.
- Added focused unit coverage for duplicate trace-name connectivity keys and preserved SRJ trace connectivity metadata.

## Why

The subcircuit/SRJ interaction is easy to misread because the same circuit-json data can represent two different things:

- a child subcircuit route that already exists and should be preserved as SRJ `traces`
- parent-board routing intent that still needs to be emitted as SRJ `connections`

With `traces[*].connectedTo` support in the autorouter, core no longer needs to trim preserved trace geometry around breakout points to make parent routing work. The preserved trace stays geometrically accurate, and the autorouter gets the same-net relationship explicitly through SRJ connectivity metadata.

For naming, the invariant is:

- generated exposed-connection helper traces should not set `name`
- user-named immediate children should not collide across different things
- same-named traces may coexist only when the connectivity map proves they are the same underlying connection
- nested subcircuits remain separately scoped

## Validation

- `./node_modules/.bin/biome format --write lib/utils/autorouting/getSimpleRouteJsonFromCircuitJson.ts lib/utils/autorouting/SimpleRouteJson.ts tests/utils/autorouting/simple-route-json-ignores-routed-subcircuit-traces.test.ts`
- `bun test tests/utils/autorouting/simple-route-json-ignores-routed-subcircuit-traces.test.ts tests/components/primitive-components/group-duplicate-trace-name-connectivity-key.test.ts`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` still fails on existing unrelated baseline errors around graph display props, `source_unnamed_trace_warning`, and the `circuit-to-svg` `convertCircuitJsonToStackedSchematicSheetsSvg` export.

Previously attempted:

- `bun test tests/subcircuits/subcircuit-exposed-nets-copper-pour01.test.tsx`

The fixture-backed exposed-net test is still blocked at module load by the existing `circuit-to-svg` export issue: `convertCircuitJsonToStackedSchematicSheetsSvg` is not exported.

title: Fix editor package visibility menu to save public/private state from package data
description: Fixes the editor package visibility menu to synchronize the visibility state with the actual package data, ensuring that the editor reflects the correct publicprivate status during updates.
body:
### Motivation

The editor page package menu could show or update package visibility independently from the actual package state. This made the editor visibility control feel out of sync with the canonical Package Settings behavior, especially when switching between Public and Private from the editor dropdown.

### Change

- Derive the editor visibility label/checkmark from `pkg.is_private` instead of keeping a separate local visibility source of truth.
- Save editor visibility using the same contract as Package Settings:
  - `public` -> `is_private: false`
  - `private` -> `is_private: true`
- Refetch the package query keys used by the editor after saving visibility.
- Disable the visibility controls and show `Changing...` while the update is in flight.

### Evidence

This matches the Package Settings visibility flow, where visibility is initialized from `packageInfo.is_private` and saved through `is_private = visibility === "private"`.

title: Fix board conversion preserving child footprint geometry
description: Fixes the issue where board conversion drops child footprint geometry from Circuit JSON generated from real TSX board code by reusing the existing generateFootprintTsx helper and emitting a child chip footprint...  when board Circuit JSON includes footprint elements.
body:
### Motivation
Board conversion currently detects `pcb_board` and emits only an empty `<board>`, which drops child footprint geometry from Circuit JSON generated from real TSX board code.

### Change
- Reuse the existing `generateFootprintTsx` helper in the board template converter.
- Emit a child `<chip footprint={...} />` when board Circuit JSON includes footprint elements.
- Add `test20` covering TSX → Circuit JSON → converter → TSX → Circuit JSON round trip.
- Update the PCB snapshot to show the preserved pads.

### Evidence
The new test starts from TSX board code with a child chip containing two SMT pads. Before the fix, the converted board rendered with zero pads. After the fix, both pads are preserved.

### Tests
```sh
bun test tests/test19-support-board-conversion.test.tsx tests/test20-board-conversion-drops-children.test.tsx

title: Add Built-In qrcode-svg Support for Circuit Evaluation
description: This PR adds built-in support for the qrcode-svg package in the evaluation runtime, allowing Circuit code to generate QR codes without requiring external module resolution.
body:
This PR adds built-in support for the `qrcode-svg` package in the evaluation runtime, allowing Circuit code to generate QR codes without requiring external module resolution. It also adds a snapshot test covering QR code rendering on PCB silkscreen graphics. 

### Changes

* Added `qrcode-svg` as a runtime dependency.
* Registered `qrcode-svg` in `builtInEvalModules`.
* Added an ESM shim so evaluated code can import `qrcode-svg` transparently.
* Added a PCB snapshot test validating QR code generation and silkscreen rendering.
* Updated the lockfile to include the new dependency.

### Why

This enables documentation examples and user circuits to import `qrcode-svg` directly inside the evaluation environment, making QR-code-based silkscreen graphics work out of the box while ensuring rendering remains stable through snapshot testing.

title: Support @tsci package imports with registry-backed type acquisition and IntelliSense in the Monaco editor
description: Fixes the issue where importing tsci registry packages in the Monaco editor resulted in a Cannot find module error and no IntelliSense support.
body:
## Problem

Importing tscircuit registry packages in the editor was broken — any `@tsci/*` import produced a false `Cannot find module (2307)` error and no IntelliSense:

```tsx
import { RedLed } from "@tsci/seveibar.red-led"      // ❌ Cannot find module
import { PushButton } from "@tsci/seveibar.push-button"
import { SmdUsbC } from "@tsci/seveibar.smd-usb-c"
```

Root cause: automatic type acquisition (`@typescript/ata`) resolves every package against jsdelivr, but `@tsci/*` packages are not published to npm — `https://data.jsdelivr.com/v1/package/resolve/npm/@tsci/seveibar.red-led` returns 404. They live in the tscircuit registry, which the editor never consulted. This made the editor effectively unusable for real tscircuit.com packages — the primary workflow it exists for.

## Fix

ATA now runs with a custom fetcher (`src/monaco/tsciPackageSupport.ts`) that intercepts the four jsdelivr URL shapes ATA constructs (version listing, version resolve, flat file listing, file content) and rewrites `@tsci/*` requests to the registry's jsdelivr-compatible endpoint, converting the scope encoding back to registry paths (`seveibar.red-led@0.0.1/index.d.ts` → `seveibar/red-led@0.0.1/index.d.ts`):

```
https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@tsci/seveibar.red-led@0.0.1/index.d.ts
→ <registry>/snippets/download?jsdelivr_resolve=false&jsdelivr_path=seveibar%2Fred-led%400.0.1%2Findex.d.ts
```

- **Non-`@tsci` traffic passes through untouched** — npm packages keep resolving from jsdelivr.
- **In-memory response cache** — type acquisition re-runs on every debounced edit; successful registry/jsdelivr responses are served from memory instead of refetched. Failed responses are never cached, so transient errors retry.
- **Configurable registry** — new `configureTscircuitTypeAcquisition({ registryApiUrl })` lets hosts point resolution at their own proxy (e.g. tscircuit.com's `/api`), keeping the component drop-in compatible. Defaults to the public registry.
- New exports: `createTsciPackageFetcher`, `rewriteTsciJsdelivrUrl`, `configureTscircuitTypeAcquisition`, `DEFAULT_TSCI_REGISTRY_API_URL`.

## Reproduction & tests

`tests/tsciPackageSupport.test.ts` (14 tests) reproduces the failure mode and locks in the fix: rewriting for all four ATA URL shapes, versioned paths and nested files, first-dot-only scope conversion, non-`@tsci` passthrough (the old 404 path), in-memory cache hits, no caching of failed responses, non-GET passthrough, and custom registry URLs.

## Verified end-to-end in the running editor

- All three example imports resolve with **zero diagnostics**; every request goes to the registry (`resolve` → `flat` → `package.json` → `index.d.ts` per package, all 200) with **no `@tsci` traffic leaking to jsdelivr**.
- Hovering `RedLed` shows the real registry-served signature: `(alias) const RedLed: (props: Props) => led`.
- Negative probe: `import { Nope } from "@tsci/seveibar.does-not-exist"` marks only that line with an error — real imports stay clean, no crash, no refetch storm.
- `bun test`, `tsc --noEmit`, and the library build all pass.

The `CodeEditor` cosmos fixture now uses the three `@tsci/seveibar.*` imports so the behavior is visible in dev.

title: Fix potentiometer KiCad pin parity
description: Fixes the vertical pin offset of the potentiometer footprint to align with KiCads CA14 footprint by using the potentiometer pitch parameter and setting the default offset to 5 mm.
body:
## Summary

- use the potentiometer pitch parameter for the vertical pin offset
- set the CA14 default offset to 5 mm
- update potentiometer footprint and KiCad parity snapshots

## Why

Pins 1 and 3 were placed at ±3.8 mm using an expression derived from the caliper size, while the matching KiCad CA14 footprint places them at ±5 mm. The existing pitch parameter was not used.

## Impact

The generated CA14 footprint now aligns all three plated holes with KiCad, and callers can customize the vertical offset through the pitch parameter.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/potentiometer.test.ts tests/kicad-parity/potentiometer_kicad_parity.test.ts`
- `bun run build`

title: fix MSOP KiCad pad parity
description: Fixes MSOP pad placement by deriving pad-row positions from body width and pad length, ensuring correct scaling and complete pad overlap in KiCad.
body:
## Summary

- derive MSOP pad-row positions from body width and pad length
- preserve KiCad’s 0.2 mm pad-to-body overlap
- update MSOP footprint and KiCad parity snapshots

## Why

The MSOP coordinate helper accepted the package body width but ignored it, placing every pad row at a hard-coded `±2 mm`. For the KiCad MSOP-8 footprint, this put both rows 0.1125 mm too close to the package center.

## Impact

MSOP pad placement now scales correctly with package and pad dimensions, and the MSOP-8 parity visualization has complete pad overlap.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/msop.test.ts tests/kicad-parity/msop8_kicad_parity.test.ts`
- `bun run build`

title: Fix TO-92S KiCad pad parity
description: Fixes the pad shapes and dimensions for the two-pin TO-92S footprint to match KiCads specifications, ensuring proper land pattern alignment.
body:
## Summary

- use a rectangular plated pad for pin 1 of two-pin TO-92S footprints
- use a pill-shaped plated pad for pin 2
- update the TO-92S footprint and KiCad parity snapshots

## Why

The two-pin TO-92S generator emitted circular pads for both pins, while the matching KiCad Heraeus footprint uses a 1.05 × 1.5 mm rectangular pin-1 pad and a pill-shaped pin-2 pad with 0.75 mm circular drills.

## Impact

The generated two-pin TO-92S land pattern now matches KiCad’s pad shapes and dimensions. Courtyard overlap improves from 89.2% to 100%.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/to92s.test.ts tests/kicad-parity/to92s_kicad_parity.test.ts`
- `bun run build`

title: fix bga topology obstacle nodes
description: Removes explicit obstacle mesh-node emission from the initial BGA topology solver, preventing the creation of unnecessary capacity nodes for blocked BGA pad regions.
body:
## Summary

Removes explicit obstacle mesh-node emission from the initial BGA topology solver.

BGA pad obstacles still produce free-layer mesh nodes where routing is allowed, but the blocked pad geometry is no longer emitted as `_containsObstacle` capacity nodes. The obstacle lists continue to flow into the downstream removal and gap-fill stages as blockers.

## Root Cause

The BGA topology generator was creating `obstacle-*` capacity mesh nodes for component pads and unmarked component obstacles. Later pipeline stages treated those nodes as normal graph regions, so the edge solver could connect them and port-point pathing could assign cost to a dense cluster of blocked BGA pad regions.

## Validation

- `bun run format`
- `bun run build`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun test tests/component-detection-bga-only.test.ts`
- Pipeline 7 probe on `example41-solar-battery-charger.srj.json`: `pcb_component_12` has `0` obstacle/red nodes at `portPointPathingSolver`

`bun test` was also run. It completed with 331 passing, 54 skipped, and 12 failing tests. The failures included expected BGA topology snapshot changes plus existing broad-suite snapshot/time-out instability outside this one-file fix.

title: Add hierarchical schematic sheet (.kicad_sch) export support
description: Adds support for exporting KiCad hierarchical schematics from Circuit JSON. When a design uses tscircuit schematicsheet (or assigns components to a sheet via schSheetName), the schematic converter now emits a root .kicad_sch containing (sheet) nodes plus one child .kicad_sch per sheet, instead of flattening everything onto a single page. Designs with no schematic_sheet elements are unaffected  they still produce a single flat file with byte-identical output. How it works Sheet plan (buildSchematicSheetPlan): computes the root  child file identities (UUIDs, filenames, page numbers) up front, so the .kicad_sch files and the .kicad_pro agree on the same identifiers. Partitioning (partitionCircuitJsonBySheet): slices the circuit-json into a per-sheet subset, then runs the existing stage pipeline unchanged on each subset  no forked rendering logic. Root-level (unassigned) content goes on the root page. Symbol instance paths: child-sheet symbols use (path rootUuidsheetNodeUuid ) (root UUID first, per KiCad); root-level symbols keep rootUuid. Root sheet_instances stays (path  (page 1)); child page numbers live in each (sheet) nodes instances, matching KiCads own format. Cross-sheet nets: same-named net labels on different sheets map to KiCad labels that connect by name across the hierarchy  the faithful representation of how circuit-json already models shared nets. No synthesized sheet pins required. .kicad_pro: the projects sheets list reuses the plans UUIDs so it matches the generated .kicad_sch hierarchy. The s-expression structure (sheet nodes, instances, sheet_instances, symbol paths) was matched against KiCads official complex_hierarchy  video demos.
body:
Adds support for exporting **KiCad hierarchical schematics** from Circuit JSON. When a design uses tscircuit `<schematicsheet>` (or assigns components to a sheet via `schSheetName`), the schematic converter now emits a root `.kicad_sch` containing `(sheet)` nodes plus **one child `.kicad_sch` per sheet**, instead of flattening everything onto a single page.

Designs with **no** `schematic_sheet` elements are unaffected — they still produce a single flat file with byte-identical output.

How it works

- Sheet plan (buildSchematicSheetPlan): computes the root + child file identities (UUIDs, filenames, page numbers) up front, so the .kicad_sch files and the .kicad_pro agree on the same identifiers.
- Partitioning (partitionCircuitJsonBySheet): slices the circuit-json into a per-sheet subset, then runs the existing stage pipeline unchanged on each subset — no forked rendering logic. Root-level (unassigned) content goes on the root page.
- Symbol instance paths: child-sheet symbols use (path "/<rootUuid>/<sheetNodeUuid>" …) (root UUID first, per KiCad); root-level symbols keep /<rootUuid>. Root sheet_instances stays (path "/" (page "1")); child page numbers live in each (sheet) node's instances, matching KiCad's own format.
- Cross-sheet nets: same-named net labels on different sheets map to KiCad labels that connect by name across the hierarchy — the faithful representation of how circuit-json already models shared nets. No synthesized sheet pins required.
- .kicad_pro: the project's sheets list reuses the plan's UUIDs so it matches the generated .kicad_sch hierarchy.

The s-expression structure (sheet nodes, instances, sheet_instances, symbol paths) was matched against KiCad's official complex_hierarchy / video demos.

title: Use descriptive identifiers in workspace editor, file sidebar, and `@tsci` package fetcher
description: Renames ambiguous single-purpose identifiers across the workspace editing path to improve code readability and intent clarity without changing behavior or functionality.
body:
## What changed

Renames ambiguous single-purpose identifiers across the workspace editing path so the code reads as what it does, following up on the `@tsci` package import support added in #12 and #13:

- **`WorkspaceCodeEditor`** — `currentFileData` → `currentEditorFile` (it's the file backing the active editor model, not arbitrary metadata), and the `OnChange` handler's `value`/`nextValue` → `updatedContent`/`nextContent` to make the echo-suppression comparison against `currentContentRef` self-explanatory.
- **`FileSidebar`** — `treeData` → `fileTree`, and the tree selection callback's `value` → `selectedFilePath`, clarifying that selection is keyed by file path before it's validated against `filesRecord`.
- **`tsciPackageSupport`** — `parts`/`transformedPackagePath` → `packagePathSegments`/`registryPackagePath` and `baseUrl` → `registryBaseUrl`, making the jsDelivr→registry URL rewrite (`owner.name` → `owner/name`) easier to follow; `text` → `responseBody` in the caching fetcher.

## Why

The `@tsci` type-acquisition and workspace-editor code paths are the ones most likely to be touched next (registry resolution, editor sync). Generic names like `value`, `parts`, and `text` forced readers to trace data flow to understand intent; the new names encode it at the declaration site.

## Notes

- Pure rename refactor — no behavior, API, or styling changes.
- `bun run typecheck` and `bun run format:check` pass; all call sites updated.

title: Add clickable `@tsci` package imports and prevent incomplete editor initialization
description: Fixes a first-load bug where Monaco reported valid relative imports as missing until the page was refreshed, and adds clickable tsci package references to enhance the editing experience.
body:
## Summary

Improves the Monaco editing experience for tscircuit projects by making `@tsci` package references clickable and introducing deterministic multi-file TypeScript initialization.

Fixes a first-load bug where Monaco reported valid relative imports such as `./MPL3115A2R1` as missing until the page was refreshed.

## Root cause

Monaco starts diagnostics as each TypeScript model is created. The active entry file was registered before its imported sibling files, allowing its first diagnostic pass to run against a partial worker graph.

Waiting for the host's file-loading flags was necessary but not sufficient: having every model in the main thread does not guarantee that every model has reached the TypeScript web worker.

## Fix

Initialization now follows an explicit order:

1. Wait for local files and package files to finish loading
2. Register non-active workspace models before the active importer
3. Explicitly synchronize every code-model URI through Monaco's public `getTypeScriptWorker()` factory
4. Acquire dependencies referenced by every code file in the workspace
5. Mount the editor only after model, worker, and type readiness match the current workspace file set

Readiness is keyed to the workspace file set. Adding or removing a file repeats worker preparation, while ordinary content edits remain debounced and do not hide the editor. No synthetic content edit or page refresh is required.

## Additional changes

- Add Monaco links from `@tsci/owner.package` references to their tscircuit package pages
- Support TypeScript and JavaScript files
- Export package-link utilities and provider registration
- Update the example circuit to exercise package imports

## Validation

- Added regression coverage for incomplete workspace states
- Added coverage for a relative module arriving after the entry file
- Added coverage that the active importer is registered after sibling models
- Added coverage that dependency acquisition scans all workspace code files
- `bun test`: 24 passing
- `bun run typecheck`: passing
- `bun run format:check`: passing
- `bun run build`: passing

## User impact

The editor no longer exposes a partially synchronized TypeScript project. Relative workspace imports resolve correctly on first load, package types are acquired across the complete workspace, and no refresh is required.

title: Add a repro test for getBoundsOfPcbElements ignoring pcb_component rotation
description: Adds a failing repro test for the incorrect bounds calculation of rotated pcb_components in getBoundsOfPcbElements
body:
Repro for #105

`getBoundsOfPcbElements` computes a rotated `pcb_component`'s bounds from its unrotated width/height, so a 4x2 component at 45deg reports +/-2 / +/-1 instead of +/-2.121 and the box no longer encloses the part.

title: use square pin1 pad for electrolytic capacitor

body:
## Summary

- use a square plated pad for electrolytic pin 1
- retain the circular plated pad for pin 2
- update the footprint and KiCad parity SVG snapshots

## Why

The generated electrolytic footprint used circular pads for both pins, while the matching KiCad footprint uses a square pad for pin 1 as its polarity marker. This caused an 8.35% pad-area difference in the parity visualization.

## Impact

Electrolytic footprints now match the KiCad pin-1 pad geometry without changing pitch, drill size, courtyard, or pin-2 geometry.

title: Fix SOT-89-3 KiCad parity
description: Fixes the SOT-89-3 footprint to match KiCad specifications by correcting lead pads, collector polygon, and courtyard dimensions, ensuring 100 courtyard IoU and proper reference designator placement.
body:
## Summary

- match the SOT-89-3 lead pads, collector polygon, and courtyard to KiCad
- move the reference designator above the footprint instead of over the collector pad
- regenerate the affected SVG snapshots and restore the parity test as a normal passing test

## Root cause

The SOT-89-3 collector was modeled as a small rectangular pad and the outer pads and courtyard were offset from the KiCad land pattern. This produced a 46.9% courtyard difference and a misleading parity snapshot.

## Impact

The generated SOT-89-3 footprint now overlays the KiCad reference with 100% courtyard IoU, while keeping the reference text clear of copper pads.

## Validation

title: Fix type-check error on main (rp implicitly any)
description: Fixes type-check error by explicitly annotating the parameter rp with the PcbTraceRoutePoint type to resolve implicit any type issue in the filter callback.
body:
`type-check` has been red , which means every PR shows a failing check. The one error:

```
src/getFullConnectivityMapFromCircuitJson.ts(37,33): error TS7006: Parameter 'rp' implicitly has an 'any' type.
```

Under the `element.type === "pcb_trace"` narrowing, `element.route` resolves to `any`, so the `.filter` callback param `rp` is implicitly `any` and `tsc --strict` rejects it. Annotating the param with the exported `PcbTraceRoutePoint` type fixes it (no `any`, no cast):

```ts
element.route.filter((rp: PcbTraceRoutePoint) => rp && rp.route_type === "wire")
```

Type-only change — the emitted JS is byte-identical, so no runtime effect. `bunx tsc --noEmit` now reports **zero** errors, and `biome format` leaves the file unchanged.

title: Fix PCB cutouts leaving copper in overlapping SMT pads
description: Fixes a subtle PCB rendering bug where board cutouts overlapping SMT pads could leave copper visible, especially when the drill color was transparent.
body:
## Summary

Fixes a subtle PCB rendering bug where board cutouts overlapping SMT pads could leave copper visible, especially when the drill color was transparent.

## Root cause

SMT-pad rendering subtracted overlapping holes, plated holes, and vias from the copper path, but did not include `pcb_cutout` elements. Cutouts were rendered later using the configured drill fill, which could visually cover copper but could not erase it when that fill was transparent.

## Fix

- Pass visible PCB cutouts into SMT-pad rendering
- Convert circular, rectangular, rounded, rotated, and polygon cutouts into subtractive pad geometry
- Filter candidates by overlapping bounds before drawing
- Use the existing even-odd fill path to remove overlapping copper cleanly
- Preserve element visibility filtering

## Impact

Mechanical board cutouts that cross SMT pads now produce accurate copper geometry instead of leaving copper inside the cutout area.

## Testing

- Added a 2x-resolution PNG regression snapshot that reproduces the transparent-cutout bug and verifies the fix
- `bun test` — 137 tests pass
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run format:check`
- `git diff --check`

title: Fix PCB cutouts failing to clip overlapping SMT pad copper
description: Fixes the issue where PCB cutouts do not properly clip overlapping SMT pad copper in the 3D viewer, ensuring accurate rendering of PCB designs.
body: ## Summary - update `circuit-to-canvas` to `0.0.111` - pass `pcb_cutout` elements into the isolated SMT pad texture render - add a reproduction story with edge cutouts overlapping top and bottom SMT pads ## Problem The 3D viewer renders SMT pads in a dedicated texture pass. That pass only forwarded pads and drill elements to `CircuitToCanvasDrawer`, so the drawer never received the board cutouts required to clip overlapping pad copper. Updating the dependency alone therefore did not activate the upstream fix. ## Fix Include board cutouts in the SMT pad texture element set. This allows the drawer to subtract intersecting circular, rectangular, and polygonal cutouts from pad copper on both PCB layers. ## Cross-repository context This completes the 3D viewer integration for [tscircuit/circuit-to-canvas#253](https://github.com/tscircuit/circuit-to-canvas/pull/253), which added the underlying SMT-pad cutout clipping support released in `circuit-to-canvas@0.0.111`. ## Before SMT pad copper remained visible across overlapping PCB cutouts. ## After PCB cutouts correctly remove overlapping SMT pad copper in the 3D viewer. ## Verification - `bunx biome check src/utils/pad-texture.ts` - `bun run build` - `bun run build-storybook` - reproduction and fix are included in the same PR

- Renames `getPcbBoundsFromCircuitJson` → `getComprehensivePcbBounds`

- Updates the internal caller (`convert-circuit-json-to-pcb-svg.ts`) and tests to the new name

description: Fixes a source-trace conversion bug where obstacle-derived same-net connectivity was being treated as required physical trace endpoints.
body: ### Summary Fixes a source-trace conversion bug where obstacle-derived same-net connectivity was being treated as required physical trace endpoints. The converter now keeps actual route endpoints in `connected_source_port_ids` and moves obstacle-derived connectivity into `connected_source_net_ids`. This preserves same-net clearance behavior while preventing `checkTracesAreContiguous` from reporting false missing-pad connections. ### Problem `convertToCircuitJson` previously appended obstacle `connectedTo` metadata into `source_trace.connected_source_port_ids`. That made DRC contiguity checks overreach: - `connected_source_port_ids` is interpreted as the list of physical ports a trace must connect to. - Obstacle `connectedTo` can include same-net ports/pads that are electrically related but are not endpoints of this routed trace. - As a result, valid routes were reported as missing connections to unrelated same-net pads. This showed up in `dataset01` as false `pcb_trace_error` failures, especially in `circuit003`.

title: Fix 3D PNG exports by enabling circuit-json-to-3d-png imports
description: Restores 3D PNG exports by adding circuit-json-to-3d-png to the dynamic importers supported modules.
body:
## Summary

Restores 3D PNG exports by adding `circuit-json-to-3d-png` to the dynamic importer's supported modules.

## Root cause

The 3D PNG export flow requests `circuit-json-to-3d-png`, but the importer rejected it as an unsupported module before attempting to load it from the CDN, even though the package is available there.

## Changes

- allow dynamic imports of `circuit-json-to-3d-png`
- add its typed module mapping and bundled declarations
- include the package in declaration synchronization
- add regression coverage for CDN URL resolution
- verify the typed importer exposes `renderCircuitJsonTo3dPng`

## User impact

Callers can once again load the 3D PNG renderer through the shared importer, unblocking the 3D PNG download flow.

## Testing

- `bun test`
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
